speaker: Rob Moore
words: I wanted to ask you again about moonshining.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How would you go and set yourself up? How would you build a still?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, you're going to put that on tape. Uh -huh. I don't know, sugar. Oh, sure.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: There's nothing wrong with that, is there?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: There ain't nothing wrong with it. I'd make some more right now if I dared to. You know, because the moonshine that I made was medicine. You know, you never have any stomach trouble if you have some of that every day.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why is that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, the stuff is made out of, you know, is made out of bran instead of other grain, you see. Bran, and that has a tendency, you know, to move your bowels, you know, that. Bran, and it's the juice out of it, of course. But I'll tell you anyway, I don't suppose you can get a, now you might get it somewhere. But, oh, wait.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: If you get these copper boilers, you know, the wash boilers used to be, you know what I mean, don't you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yes. You can make one right out of that. Or you can have it made, you know, by some pin smith.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, what, okay, you have a boiler, and what do you do with that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, you see, you've got the boiler, but of course you can have them made, you see, this other stuff too. Might be a little handier than the wash boiler. But you see, you take the lid and you have a, you cut a hole in the middle of the top of the lid, you know. And you have, of course, I can see, you know, the little sardine, that sardine made a funnel, you know, set on that. And then run over here, you know, and run out, and then they...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Wait, is it like a, is that where you use the copper tubing?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, then it runs into the copper tubing, you see.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, from the top of the boiler?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: From the top of the boiler, yes, in there, you know, and it runs through that. On that you have a wooden keg, or any kind of a metal or anything for water, you know. That whole water, that water runs in there to keep cool, you see, that's what...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And the coil runs through that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, the coil runs through the water. And of course the water runs in there, you've got to keep that cool, gets too hot. And the pipe ridge, you know, will make the moonshine. See, what it does is it steams, it makes it, you see, and runs through that water, condenses, and runs down into your vessel. Moonshine is getting stronger first.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What is double run moonshine?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Double run means it, say, puts your mash in a barrel, you know, wooden barrel. But you're taking this all down here. You see, you should take your 50 gallon barrel, wooden barrel, and take a smaller one as far as that is concerned. But anyway, you should take your gas and helium in it, and set it apart. You know, in the barrel. And jar it a little all around. Of course, you have a little counter burner over it too. But, so that gives it, and keeps it from tasting like wood, you know. So you put it down the barrel, your mash, long enough, without having it charred, and it gets to tasting woody, you know. Oh. Kind of sour like, and something. And, but that's where you char it. And then, of course, then, of course I kept every hole about. I built a little shack, you know, out of logs.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Mm -hmm. I don't remember. But you've got an idea. Uh -huh. A little shack out of logs, you know, took corners in there, you know. Two by four and nail them together. Yeah. Corners, you know, and of course you can build a little log shack any way you want to. That is the easiest way, you know. Cut your logs just the right length. You see, you can put them in there, and nail in these two by fours in there, you know. Oh, I see. On the end. Uh -huh. That's the material because you can make it out of lumber or anything in a little shack. Mm -hmm. Of course, you can get out any, without a shack. Yeah, you know. Oh, you know, make your own time. Of course, you've got to have it, say, in the summertime. If there's outside, then you have your little bench, you know. See your barrel's setting up. Mm -hmm. Put a lantern just under this bench, you see. Well, no, wait. There's a little thing, you know, that built up, and set the barrel on top, but it had a place under there to stick the lantern under, you know. And turn it down so you turned on the flow fast and get too hot. And, uh, you know, you're in a nice way, you know. That's before you put it in the still. Yeah. Oh, and when it sours so long.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long is it supposed to sour? Well, tell me, what's working?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh. And, uh, the way to do it is a brand, I can tell you. 50 pounds of sugar to a 50 -gallon barrel. And about 3 gallon of brand. Mm -hmm. And then you each, you know, make it work. Of course, now the first, of course, I don't know how it worked. I never tried it. I only did two different times. But, uh, of course, you can put corn in there, too. You can give it to corn, whatever, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you usually do that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: After I got wise to it, I did. Oh. Of course, I started, you know, from about one till, uh, and then worked up my different one, you know. And then the double run, you see, it's safer. So here you take it out of the barrel. You know, that there, you strain that through a gunner sack or anything, you know, into your still.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, so you take all the solid stuff out.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: And, uh,

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: then you run through, you see, first. Mm -hmm. Till it gets down, well, just about all the alcohol out of the, gets to come and say you'd have a little tester. Mm -hmm. See, if you used to, I don't know if you can get them now or not. But that was during the prohibition, so you can get testers in the drugstore.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Tester.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why is that? Why do you think they didn't, you could do that? If you went and bought a tester, wouldn't people know you were going to buy it to make moonshine with? Yeah, but who cared them?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh. You know, everybody was making moonshine. It was about, you know, after I got caught for moonshine, I found out all my neighbors was making it, too.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, really?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. I heard, I was back to all of them. So, but, uh, there was just two or three of us got caught. Of course, there's, couldn't go into a long, make a long story out of it. I was going to, about that. But anyway, you run it through, you see, to get, putting your, all the alcohol out of it. You see, and it's probably a test of 70 percent. Then, that's first run, you see. Well, maybe that ain't right. But it's close. Anyway, but anyway, then you put it aside, you see, you put it in a vessel to hold it. I had a big 20 gallon crock, you know, jar. And I put that in, you see, then I'd run the other barrel, if I had two barrels set at once. And, well, now I'll just go from the one. Instead of taking the two, take this here, you see, and reduce it with water, plain water, to 60 percent.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: 60 percent or 60 proof?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: 60 proof. Oh, okay. And so, if you get it much stronger than that, then your lad will blow your still up. Oh! See, then you can run it again, put it in there, you see, 60 proof, and run it again. So, it's, you know, heat, you got heat. I had a little oil stove that I had to use to run mine with. Of course, you could run it on a cook stove or anything, you know. Like the old timers, I don't know. Might have trouble running it on an electric cook stove. But, anything, you know, gas, now you can run the gas on one of these gas stoves, too. Heat it, you see. Not running too fast, anytime, but just about so. And then you get it down to, well, the way I did it, it was 100. You see, after I was level running it, back to 100, got, so as I'd catch some of that in that little tube, you know, and test it to 100. Then I'd cut that off, and run that, the rest of that, you know, I'd cut that off there, it'd be 140. And then I'd reduce it down to 100. I had two testers, you know, one was a government tester, and the other was that little tube tester, you know. Of course, if it was a government, you could tell her what she was doing by it. Of course, you could reduce the type of thing about the big some down there that date, and there was no harm about, you know, it wasn't strong enough. But I'd test it, you know, in the cold. And then I'd test it to 100, you see. That tube didn't burn, you know, like most strong whiskey, and he thought it was too weak. And he was, I didn't dare tell him to know that I was making it. But I told him, I seen the fellow test it. He tested, he said, the way, that's the way I tested, you see. It was down in the basement, and it was cold. And I tested it to 100 then, you see, with that tube. And of course, after he had it normal, like it was still like it was supposed to be, I tested about 118. And he didn't think it was a test. But of course, then I learned a lot of that after talking to him, you see. And of course, as he went along, he learned more, you know. It was just wonderful. And of course, I had to color it. Well, I'd reduce in this here, you know, cut it off at 100, you know, tested 100. Uh -huh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What would you thin it out with?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, good, clear spring water, you know. Oh. Of course, I had it over there where I was running it into. I tested down with that to 100, you see. It tested 100 with that pair. It would test there. And I got that double run. It would test 140, you know. Test 140 cool. Uh -huh. And I'd reduce it down and I'd see it to the 100. Uh -huh. With the spring water. Of course, I had the government tester there, too. And that showed you a lot different than that other one, you see. It didn't matter whether you're liquor was warm or cold. I'd give you the right test to it, you see. The government tester did. Of course, that was hard to figure out. The government tester now.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you say you used to color it also?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, I colored it with, of course, the scents. My brother was up Spokane and I found out, you know, the thing that knew it, it colored to make it taste like the old whiskey, you know. Oh. I think, you know, what made the old color and everything was the keg you just put in, you see. Uh -huh. See, you put it in a barrel. It could be either way. I guess they're all charred, you know. You put it in there and they would, you know, yearn and they're charred. But, see, this was beating the time. Uh -huh. See, by taking the oak shavings, you know. Uh -huh. And scorching them. Uh -huh. And putting them in, well, see, you had that big jar, you know. 20 gallons, probably 15 gallons. Uh -huh. And put in, you know, probably a gallon, you know, about a half a gallon of them shavings, you know, and then parched the oak shavings, you know. Uh -huh. You can buy them by the gunning sack, though, in Spokane. Uh -huh. My brother, he sent it up there for him, you know, to get one and send it down. Sack, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, was he a brother in moonshine, or two?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He knew how. He was in moonshine. He made some once in a while, you know, for his own use, but he didn't try to make a business out of it. See, the reason I was making a business out of it, heck, he couldn't get any work, you know. And then, I guess, it started in 29, I think, or something like that. And then I started to making it at that time. Let's see. I don't remember just what time, but...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: But anyway, I'd sell it, you know, five dollars a gallon. Uh -huh. Of course, there was others, but that's the reason I got caught, because I was selling the air balloons for four dollars, you see. And then I'd buy mine for five, and preference to that, and the other for four.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were there a lot of people who were making moonshine for sale?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. Well, gosh, there was no work. Uh -huh. If you cut wood, you might get a fuse cord to cut, but you know, if you worked in the sun, you know, maybe you'd get two days a week, two dollars a day. If you could get it, you know, you couldn't buy it, you know, get work. Uh -huh. Just a fuse, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, how much money could you make off of one batch of moon?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, I got something that I could run about six gallons out of one barrel. It might be thirty dollars, and you got it, but you had to buy your sugar. Uh -huh. And you ran your yeast and the jugs and the first one, like, another. Uh -huh. And I'd do it pretty well then, you know, for just a common, you know, for living, as well. Living in a van of new debts, you see.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, how long would it take you to make that batch?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, I tell you, you take after you get started, you could get a barrel, a mini tray, to go in about four days or less. Sometimes, depending on the little circumstance, a little bit. About four days, see, and then you'd run that off. You know, you used to take one barrel, you see, you'd run that off, and double it back in about one day. Uh -huh. Of course, you see, the night and the morning didn't make any difference to me, you see. I could go to bed and sit in the front, you know, with the alarm, and go off, and so I could go to sleep, you see, and get up and go and tend to it at the same time, you see. Uh -huh. Of course, if you wanted to start it, make it some, you'd come back again, and you'd write it all down, you know. Uh -huh. Okay. Well, you'd have it here with this, I'd jump around so darn much, but my dog could mix you up.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, was there a lot of competition between different moonshiners?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Not an awful lot. There's just a few crooked moonshiners, you know, that didn't make it. I'd just, one fella, he tricked, but he didn't make it at that. One fella, you know, he got jealous, his one shiny thing. He had trouble with selling it for four dollars. Uh -huh. And they'd buy mine right out and honor him, you know, at five dollars. So he figured a scheme, you know, and had his boy to get a friend, you see. There was one fella there, a Scotsman, him and I didn't get along at all, you know. Uh -huh. He had a boy of, I don't know, 18, 16, 18 or 17 or 18. Uh -huh. So this other fella was a moonshiners boy, you know. Of course, the old man himself was the father of the deal. Uh -huh. See, we got him, you know. Well, he was a snooper, you know. He looked around and he told me one day, he says, every time he says you move, you're still. I'd move it in the summertime, you know. It wouldn't take long for him to move it or anything, you know. Uh -huh. He said, every time you move, you're still. He said, I knew where it was at. Uh -huh. He had the snoop to find it, you know. Yeah. I never snooped around to see where he thought it else was. But, uh, so then he got this, his boy, his old boy, to take this 17 or 18 year old boy, you see. Told him where my tail was. They run right down down to it, you see. Uh -huh. Of course, he knew where it was, you see, but.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, who, now who was this that ran right into it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: It was the, the other moonshiners boy. Uh -huh. And, uh, then that's your Scotsman boy, you know. Oh. He figured that if the Scotsman got a little bit better all over the world, you know. And, uh, of course, I guess he didn't know, but that wasn't where I got caught.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, wait, no, no, what did they do when they got there? Did they take your still or take the moon or what?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: They took, poured the moon out over the sun with it. They took a gallon of the clear 140 proof stuff. Uh -huh. And then I had about a little over half a gallon already fixed up the drink. Uh -huh. They took that and they, well, they see it in nothing. They're not even honest about the darn thing anyway, you know. You see, up there that, uh, let's see, Kellogg. Uh -huh. Wasn't exactly right, I think. Up there, there was a creek that called it Moon Creek. Uh -huh. Yeah, I read it, it called it Moon Creek. There was a moon shining there going on. And, uh, so the fellow there, there was an Italian, well, maybe three or four of them, and they would build a house there, you know, and hold probably a hundred of them barrels, you know. Uh -huh. And they, they hipped up, you know, in there. Didn't have to have a hundred hundred, you know, if you had a farm enough to, uh, to let it work, you know. Uh -huh. So they had that farm enough to work. And they, uh, had the, uh, they shared federals, you see. They was protected, you know, by these federals. Uh -huh. To clear from the moon, Moon Creek in there, clear into Spokane.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How were they protected? Well, they, uh, what do you call them darn thing?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: They were moonshine hunters.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The Revenuers?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, the Revenuers. They were the guys seeing the detective. Well, now, say I wanted to, at that time, I wanted to, you see, of course, up my size so I can afford paid protection, you know, with the moon. Now, now, now it's making, I was gonna say, paid thirty -five dollars a day protection, you see, there. One had paid moon. Well, that was up at Moon Creek, they did that. And one time, see, after they'd used it, and left it, they left all their barrels there, but, see, they run,

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: knowing it got mixed up, it wouldn't matter anyway. See, like, this, three thousand gallon or something, got them on there, run there. But that time, out, that's the only one. Protected about thirty -five days after they got started, you know. But anyway, if you wanted protection, you see, you know who these Revenuers was, you know. When you go to one of them, you know, in town, people like some protection. Oh, no, you're a dog -robin' feller. You pull around me, you say, I'll have you put in jail. Now, you say, if you don't believe it, you go over here and see Bob Moon. He'll tell you. So, you go over there and see Bob Moon, and he'll tell you how much moon you want to make. Tell him. And he'll tell you how much you had to pay, you see, to make it so long. Oh, I see.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So they wouldn't be directly involved with it? Huh? So they wouldn't be directly involved with it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, you see, you couldn't prove anything from them veterans.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, did that happen around here, too?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, that happened, yeah, a lot of places. I don't know just where it was around here, but I know about that and up at Moon, you see, because I was married by a niece that lived right there on Moon Creek. Ah, uh -huh. He wasn't the one making this, uh, we call them dagels. Uh -huh. But you know what, they was either off -bridge or kind of in there. Got into it, seemed like they could get into things like that and get out of it, but they'd go even at that, even at that. But a lot of times, you see, the revenuers would double -cross them and catch them anyway. Uh -huh. Have an outsider come in. Uh -huh. You know, they was friends, you see, both ways.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, who were the revenuers here in Moscow?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, there wasn't no revenuers really, but there was a fellow here. He was a veteran man, but he told me, he said, I don't look for moonshiners. He said they sign a warrant for me and I'd take them in, some, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you remember his name? Do you remember his name?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, Bob West. Ah. He owned his whole hill here once. You know, all the houses weren't. It was out of the land, too.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was he the man who arrested you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, the one who arrested me was from up in Coeur d 'Alene, up in Kellogg. One of them was, and they haven't blown the cook. And their name. Uh -huh. See, I forget their names.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were they accompanied by a local sheriff, too? Huh?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, yes, they were accompanied by a sheriff here.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Which one was that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: That was Charlie Summerfield. Oh. He was a good guy, too. Uh -huh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, how was it that you came to be arrested? How did that happen?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, well, see, there was a fellow here who was running a restaurant down here on Third Street. And a moonshine, or selling moonshine on the side, you see. Uh -huh. And, I never seen...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Who was that? Who was that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I don't know of some of his relations around here yet. Oh, okay.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: But anyway, why?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I don't think it makes any difference at this point. You know, some of these folks might not have ever known about him. Being in the moonshine. They'd probably be proud of him by now, you know. They might be, but chances are there's a lot of people that's queer, you know. Uh -huh. In my shape, you know, if I'd made it and got caught and put the sergeants, they wouldn't yell on the bench, you know, if they'd ever been jailed. Uh -huh. But me, that happened.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What's the use in lying about it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Right, exactly. But anyway, I'd like to have some of that. Old moonshine is about five dollars. I'll sell my stomach, you know. Well, you see, at first, it started there, then I started making it. Well, there was some people down at Coors made it first, you see.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Down at where?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: At Coors, Washington. Uh -huh. That's down this side of Pasco, probably 50 miles or more. And I guess it would have been more than 55, 56, or 57 miles above Pasco, you see, is that dusty country. Uh -huh. And anyway, they had it down there. They'd run off 15, 20 gallons before harvest, you know. Uh -huh. So we had one kind of old harvest. You know, they'd take a drink in the morning, and drink at noon, and drink at night, pour supper, you know. But anyway, I had elzers, you see, and I had to lurch that, you see, and it was up here. And I started making some, you know. Well, they worked without any of them. Uh -huh. They had a lot of camps, they had potlatch, all of them, just about, every one of them shut down at that time. And so I was making a little moonshine, you know, and I had elzers. You know, you know what, they're, they get in your stomach. Yeah. They don't cure good. Yeah. But you know, that darn moonshine cured that up. Of course, then I went from here, oh, I had a little battle of all. And he knew about me making this moonshine, and I thought I'd better get out. Uh -huh. So I went up, uh, at Syesheen Lake, I guess you know how that's... Yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. I was up there, and a friend I used to know, you see, he was doing a little bit of wood, business of honing wood to Spokane.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Uh -huh. So I got a job cutting a little wood. Uh -huh. But it snowed so deep that you couldn't work. So I went down to the barge and money on the fella to live on. Uh -huh. You know, in the batch, you didn't take too much to batch. Uh -huh. And, uh, and later on that winter, you know, I killed here for meat. You know, you killed air in the air anytime, but you're, as far as I know, concerned about having too much tension. But that was during that hard time, you see. Uh -huh. One of them fell on the other side of the lake the next year, and he'd come up for moonshine. Uh -huh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: But he asked a game warden, he says, what did you do? Because the year that I was in there, he asked me, he said, what did you do? He said, have you caught me with a deer?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Uh -huh. I was seeing him in the wintertime. Well, he says I've arrested you.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Didn't he say?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He knew. But anyway, he was saying to him nothing good. Uh -huh. Right. Yeah, it's time you got done, what he said.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So, there, well, that was the second one, right?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Uh -huh. I had to make a six -gallon thing, you know, at the monkey warden. And I guess, sure enough, I was making them days, you know, especially for the little moonshiners.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, and you could buy them through the catalog?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. Only it was different, you had to work them over a little bit, you see. Uh -huh. It was six gallons, you know, of the slip -on lid, you know, and then you put a little rag around there with a little dough. And it's in baked tights, you know. Yeah, it was a six -gallon, and then it had a...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, wouldn't it, since liquor was illegal, wouldn't it be illegal to sell the things to make liquor, too? Then?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Uh -huh. Well, that's what there's an after you for. Oh. That's what the pencil you for was selling the liquor.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: No, no, I mean, wouldn't it be illegal for Montgomery Wards or somebody to sell stills?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, not to sell that, no, and then sell those stills. They just sold it on alpetta papers so you could make a still out of it easy enough. Oh, I see.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When you're building six gallons, you'd take it quite a while, wouldn't it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: You'd run off a 50 -gallon barrel, but it wouldn't take you 14 or 15 hours to actually know that little thing. And a boiler, why, you could do a lot better with that, you see, the boiler would weigh, or hold 14 gallons, you know, at that time. You could buy that and put the thing on there, you know, and start it on the boiler stove. Leave it for a water stove and I have six hours. You could leave it and have it run off a single, you see. And you'd go up and change it, you see, for that. Put it there in the other vessel and put on a new batch, you see. Time again, you'd have it six hours, you see. About 18 hours, it would run off with the barrel filled with ash.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, you were starting to tell me about how you got arrested.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, yeah, well, the way it was, this fellow here in town, you see, he was running around, he had a partner, and the partner was doing the moon, or bootlegging, you see, the moonshine. So, he sold it to a, to a kid over by Garfield, I guess it was. Over there, and he thought he got drunk, you know, the kid did, I got up, thought he was drunk, I suppose, you know. He was only 18 years old or 17, something like that. So, the officials got a hold of him, you know.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: So, they told him that if he'd come to get the fellow that sold it to him, that'd end pretty, you know. Well, he did help me see him, this fellow. And they caught this fellow, sold it to him, and gave him six months in this county jail here, you see. In the courthouse. Well, I don't know what the same hell of a matter with him, but they got him down in, they bought him there in the sheriff's office, you see. And the quiz man, you know, and he told every fellow that he'd ever heard of in Macon moonshine, told the officials, you see. And of course, these fellows was around waiting, you know, for them to round up somebody, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You say you were the only one right here in Moscow?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, right around .... There was quite a few of them around on the other side of Kendrick, going around south, walking in there.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: And, let's see, several of them got searched here, but they had a little stuff on it, you know. Well, it's swimming, fellas. They put a framing on me, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, he was the bootlegger?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He was moonshine, too. Well, you see, they got me first, and then they went after him, and they found him up in the woods, you see. He had three boys or something like that. And one boy, you know, one probably stayed down there at the house. The other two or three went up there, and that's where the moonshiner was, you see. So this one kid, he was up there with him, you see, he went along and along. He ran in, the whole area had moonshine, but they didn't have it. Well, the light, you see, the officials had got there, you know, and told her what was after you, see. Well, he was gone, she had four or five gallons of moonshine already made, and you know, it was upstairs. And she slipped up and poured it out. You know, it went down through the house there, and got somewhere. Anyway, then the fellow that had caught the mace, you see, by the game, this here darned kid, he slipped in the hedge, you know, and upset the mace, barely on the head, and probably running on these 10 or 12 gallon mace, you know. Little cake, you know. And he upset it, and of course it mixed in some pickle brine, I think. Just anyway, to get rid of it, you know, get it poured into something that they got some of that wasn't enough to convict him on.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, were you at your still house when...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, yeah, I was right there just finishing up there. I guess I had about 15 gallons of that. Well, no, maybe not, quite that much. 10 or 12 gallons of that, 140 proof stuff, just to put in and get it out of there. Of course, there was little circumstances that come up where I would have had it rolling out two or three days ahead of that. And then another thing was too, see I was married to a different wife to this one, she had a daughter. And the daughter, I had the house, you know, could that open the window, you know, just open it some, just on hinges, you know, and catch sale. And that was at the back of the house, and this daughter, her daughter, she was 20 I guess, no maybe 21 or 22. When she told her, she told her mother, she says, I can jump out this window and run down and tell Dad. I didn't see if that would happen, but she was looking out for her own skin, the old baby was. So she was afraid that the girl might get into trouble, you see, because of her. So she didn't come, but always appreciated it. She thought about it. Yeah, she thought about letting me know, you know, she didn't want me, I guess I could get her right in the trap, you know. They told her where I was at, of course, she didn't know that I was right in there, but you know, I was 50 yards out, you know, I was cutting some poles to make a meat house out of, you know. And she told her right where I was at, and of course all they had to do was cut down by the windows out there for anything. Why wouldn't they have been making any noise in there, you know, rattling tin, stuff, you know, they had the buckets, you know, they filled that stuff out of one place and another, you know, they mashed. I think that month, he heard, you see, I had that little cabin made, you see, out of logs, covered over a branch, I was cutting a branch, you know, trees and trimming them up and on top of that little cabin. I had a place there, you know, that I could sneak in.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: So he heard me in there, he seen that place there, and he went in, come in, I told you that before, though, he said, how you doing?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I said, well, I was doing pretty good, you go along.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Who was that that came in and said that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: That was Federal Man.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was that West?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, no West, he, as I told you, he never went out. Oh, that's right, that's right. He just, they'd swear out a warrant, you know, for him to come and get, hit us on everything, I guess. Swear out a warrant to him, and then he'd go get the men. He was, he killed one Negro here in town when he lived here someplace around, one of these houses, you see. He was, he was up there, he'd take that Negro to death, knock him down, it was about a horse, a Negro had a horse, you know, a running horse. And, of course, he was a southerner, you see, this last West. You know, they hated Negroes. That was the reason they were staring at us. Negro had a horse out on the pasture, you know, just roped him out, you know, to, then West come along and said something to him, he was a sass -wrest, and West jumped on to him. He was a big man, too, I guess he hit him quite well up his shelf, knocked him down, and jumped on to him trying to get to him.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, did West lose his job for that, or ever going to stand trial for it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Nope, it went right on.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did he ever stand trial for it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I don't think so, never heard of it. Of course, I was out in the country, you see, in them days, I guess it was in the papers about it, but I doubt it. Probably didn't do anything, but, you know, it used to be lawful, I guess, to kill somebody and get by with it. Sometimes, you know, something like that. Well, law probably wasn't enforced very well. Especially, you know, Hyrton was an ego that got killed by it. It wasn't nothing fair about it, because he was a nice old negro, you know. Old gray -haired fellow, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you know Hat Moody? Huh? Do you know Hat Moody?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, Hat was telling us about moonshining days, and he was saying that there really wasn't much hard feeling between the cops and the moonshiners. And that was his opinion.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think that was true?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, that was true.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: You know, of course, they spread them in, they was out to get, you know, their reputation up, you know, to keep it. But, you see, you just take the common law, like the sheriff, some of them, of course, I didn't do it, but there was some of them, you know, take the sheriff take a gallon of, moonshine, and give him a little advantage, you know, by letting them know it, when the federal was around the country. Uh huh. And, uh, and some of them said, I had a friend of yours, he was in on, you know, he was a cop. One time he phoned me up, he says, his hunters, of course, this is in the middle of summer, he says, his hunter's out in the country now. But I wasn't making no money. But he didn't know what he was, he told me, he says, his hunter's out here in the country. I told him, heck, I says, ain't no game, hope. He said that no matter, he says, his hunters. Uh huh. I know what he meant. There was bootleggers, or not bootleggers, but, uh, not them real, they were stool pigeons. Oh. You know, looking.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were there a lot of stool pigeons around?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, there was a lot of them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: See them?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: One that had that handsome boy and that other fella.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Come to mind, then, why don't you go in with the stool pigeons?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, did stool pigeons get paid for turning people in?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, I don't think so. Not them, then. There was one stool pigeon over at Troy. You see, he was kind of lucky. See, when I was a kid, I was about 16. There was a rolling bunch, you see, there was one barber here and down in Mississippi. He usually worked in the harvest fields and was around in the saw mill. And there was a bunt roll, they called him.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: They called him what?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Bunt roll. So he, when I was about 16, I ain't sure if he was 15, no, I must have been 16, he borrowed a dollar on me.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: So, that went on there for probably 20 years. And I moved down to Troy for a while there, you see, and was working in the widget. I thought it was just as long to be there as Troy is anywhere. And of course I had a place 10 acres out till then, but that's too far for me. I worked, of course, I had to go to the problem camp and stay all the way and then come home Saturday night. But, anyway, what was I gonna...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Talk about Roland?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh yeah. So, he was bootlegging over there from a running service station. He made his headquarters there. Bootlegging was going over town.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Is that Carl Olson's service station?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I see Carl Olson. I see Sam Olson. Oh. It was Troy then. He's in Deary now. He was alright, Sam was.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That was his gas station? Huh? That was his gas station?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, he was in the service station, you see. And that's where he kind of made his headquarters. I don't know where he kept his boots. But anyway, you see, it had been 20 years since I'd seen him. Since he burned out $20, $21. And so when he seen me there in Troy, you know, in the yogurt across the street, hey, this! Kind of short. Otherwise, you know, before that, he could see me in town, lost him before he had to be a bootlegger. And he didn't even know me for that dollar. Yeah. I told myself, now, there's something the matter with that fella. You know, every time he'd see me across the street or anywhere, he'd yell at me and take me and give me two or three drinks. You know, among Jack. Of course, I didn't buy one. So I thought, well, there's something fishy about that fella. So I run on, I was in Harris, and I got out of Harris and was right there in Troy, you know. He'd been running on me in a hurry, you know. Old beakery, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He says, say, he says, you know where I can get two gallons of whiskey?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He says,

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I think it was selling at $10 a gallon then. And he says, I've got to sail for two. He said, I better get mine at Jeannie. See, that's below Spokane there, I guess you could say.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Cheney, Washington?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, Cheney, Washington. That was a big... I had a cousin, my second cousin, I guess it was, there was... on the coat. But he was a big shot. Education, you see. Mm -hmm. But anyway, he says, I ain't got my... He says, I might as well lose the sail if I don't get it right away. That other stuff was hanging in my crow, you know, there was something the matter with that fella. Yeah. So I told him, I said, I don't know, I did know where I could have got it in 15, 20 minutes. But I said, I've just been out in Harris, and I said, I don't know much of the business around here. I went over to this Sam Olson, you know. I says that compound, Bunt Rowan is a stewbridge, I guess you could call it. He says, he sells more whiskey than anybody in the country. So I don't know, it was about two weeks I went by there. I ain't just sure where I'd been, I worked a little bit out somewhere then. I hadn't been around there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I went by the service station there, you know, he said, how did you know that Bunt Rowan was a stewbridge?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I never did tell him why I knew, but I didn't think I was smart. But he turned in too. See, at that same time he was trying to get me, you know, to get him some moonshine. He didn't figure out I was making it, but he knew that I could get it. That's why he thought it didn't matter at the end. But of course, the way it was, you see, the officials here in Moscow told him, you know, the sheriff and, I don't know, some fellow men or something told him, you know, he could move the bootleg just as much as he wanted to, but he had to turn in somebody once in a while, you know, moonshine. So he was free, you know, he was open with that bootleg.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That's kind of a way of getting protection then, isn't it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. Of course, I don't, well, you see, too, though, this other one was the barber here.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: See, they'd, uh, been somebody, he had a nephew that was a moonshiner, too, you see. So he got caught, I think, seven gallons, all in, in his premises somewhere, you know, this barber. And, I forget what the thing, $100 or something like that. I told my brother, I said, well, you guys should probably want to look out for him for this rolling, you see.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, this is also rolling? The barber was rolling?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, he was a different rolling.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He was a brother to the other one. Oh. But he was a different one, he was E. P. Roan and Bunt Roan and George Roan and I don't know just how many Roans there was of that tribe. And then had some nephews, you know, and things. But they had nephews. So, I think I'm sure enough, in a day or two, or maybe a week, by this nephew, he got caught. For giving him six months in the county jail, you see. Well, how come, how come you, oh, go on, I'm sorry.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Huh? What were you going to say?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I don't know. Oh, I'm sorry, I interrupted you.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How come you got sent to McNeil Island instead of to the county jail?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, you see, all of them had, got caught at the same time as I did. They was up, they could take their choice. They could have, you know, they could get a lawyer for free. Or, they could have their own lawyer and they were free, but they, see, well, I'll tell you. I don't know what they can do, but, I won't say. Anyway, sure.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What were you going to say?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I'm going to tell you about, see, the reason I got the year in the day I came to get to the island.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, the reason you ended up on McNeil's Island?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, it was, see, I could have had it, I was, you see, my wife there, the wife I had then, because she'd had cancer in the liver and she died. And I wouldn't have, I wouldn't have.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: She died before you got arrested?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. No, she died after I got arrested. Oh. I was out on bail when she died and she had cancer, you see, in her liver and everything. It was just a mess of stuff, I guess. But anyway, she died and, of course, I had to pay the operation and the funeral. Everything, well, heck, I didn't have no money. But there was a darn fellow living in town that I had lived with for quite a while and I never did think much of his dad. Because, you see, he was up at the courthouse one time and his dad was...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: And, you know, I always figured a person had a right to be whatever he wanted to in politics, you know. And he was making fun of somebody, you know, and talking about somebody, you know. He was a vote, vote, socialist, you know, and making all kinds of fun, you know. And I thought then I liked that boy pretty well before that. And I heard him and going on, you know, and him and two or three other big shots, you know, laughing and talking about this for a vote of a socialist. And it ended with him. I didn't hear anything by him. And I figured that because I figured it was his business. Yeah, right. However, he wanted to vote, he had a right to vote that way. And so it was his boy, though, that sent word for me to come and save you after I got caught and was out on bond. So I went and seen this repugnant, he told me, he said, I can save you three months at a time.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: His boy was a lawyer? His boy was a lawyer?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, this fellow, boy was a lawyer, you see, and he sent for me to come and save you, see. And I went to his office the other afternoon and just flew around waiting to be sentenced, you know. So he told me, he said, now, he said, I can save you three months, he says, and wait until you get out of this before you pay me. He paid me $75.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: And I turned to him, you know, I said, I'm going to take you on the nose. And so, of course, I could see, you see, after I went through it, where he was willing to wait, you know, and depend on him because he wouldn't be there on 10 or 15 minutes, you know. And for $75 and wait until I got it, it would have saved me three months. Well, them had a lawyer, you see, appointed him, appointed for him. Or anyway, you see, them had a lawyer, they got nine months at a road camp. And then I was on the nose, I got a year and a day in the penitentiary, you see. But the fellow that told me, he was a good friend of mine, I didn't want to say nothing about who his name was, you know.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: But he was a friend of mine, of course, he knew that, I guess he knew that I was a moonshine boy. I got caught.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Anyway, he was a sheriff. And he don't...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, is that Summerfield? How did I know?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Okay. Go on. And so he told me, he says, well, he said, you can do as you please, but he says, I advise you to take him on the nose. He says, you'll be out, he says, before any of these fellows that's got a lawyer will be. They'll put them in the road camp and you'll get a year in the penitentiary with you, get out a month before they do.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, and good behavior or something like that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Good behavior and everything, you see. So I took a break, pardon my nose. So I just like one day of being six months from the day I was sentenced here, until I was back in Moscow.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Huh, only six months?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, not quite. Just one day, like one day of being six months.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, what was it like with Neil Allen?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, I'll tell you, it was all right on it, it was a little dangerous.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: In what way?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, in this way, you know, there's all kinds of people in there, you know. Some were murdered and they all meet an insane missile and they'd strike, you know. And of course up here, all around up here, there's a big cage up here, you know, and up there is a machine gun. The guard, then all along they'd walk on the outside there. Another boy with a couple of six shooters on him, going back and forth, but of course that wasn't dangerous there. Him. But you see, some of these here were real mean ones, they didn't give a cuss whether they'd pop in for so long as life was not so long. They might have started something, you see, and got that brother with the machine gun turned loose up there. Hard to tell who would have got killed. That was the only thing I didn't like about it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You know, it was that darn thing, huh?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: They collapsed, they had the habit of having them strikes, you know. They liked the food or something, you know, heck, the food was good. Yeah, good. Hadn't any of it. But you know, the mean ones, real mean. The fellas, you know, they wanted to raise heck anyway.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, when you got there, did you have to have a couple of fights or something to show you or...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, if you had a fight, they'd put you in a dungeon without anything but bread and water to eat down in the dark. No, you didn't dare to fight hardly, of course you would if you got some adenine for it. Yeah. It wasn't that much to do, but it wasn't enough to fight on some of it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did you do to pass your time in there?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, they, uh, checkers or dominoes or you could read or anything, you know. You could get any book they had. All kinds of books, you know, that you could send and tell them what you wanted. And you could write it on the table, you know what you wanted. And get any book you wanted to read, you know. Not in there, but in there. They just booked what you wanted anywhere you knew about, you know. And you could get it for you and everything. Of course, they'd let you write once a month, I think. And for instance, their paper and stamp and envelope and everything. And of course, they'd always read your letter that you wrote. And if you had a letter come, I'd always read it before you got it. But that didn't matter, you know, you wouldn't be writing anything. It wasn't going to hurt anybody anyway. Oh, it was pretty good. I had some good friends in there, too, you know. They, of course, learned something there, though. They were probably with them, you know, because I had known about these folks getting sent to the penitentiary. I thought, well, they're just unlucky, you know, down there. But I found out, of course, that every darn one of them was in there, kept there for a life.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Really?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, moonshiners or bootleggers or something like that wasn't, but, you know, the same amount of them. There was a third cousin of mine in there. He had the highest IQ that was in the, see, there was about a thousand in there when I was in there. He had the highest IQ of anybody in the penitentiary.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: But you know what he was in there for?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Some way, I don't know, he'd rob orphans. You know, some kind of a deal, fire orphans, and he'd rob them. Of course, you see, you take a fellow like him now, you see the woman, she was a woman, the one that was giving out the pearls. She thought that fella was just big because he was so well educated.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Me?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I walked in there on the shoulder and I see him. I walked in, but she was looking for that fellow. Oh, she said, in the couple of looks, you ain't the man I want to look at. See, I knew what was his education that made her think he was something, but, you know, that was something that I wouldn't have more thought about doing. Anything that he knew was robbing orphans. It was all right. He tried to make up to me, but I didn't make up to him. I didn't want nothing to do with him. Because, you know, a fellow would do that. Of course, I guess maybe Phil ain't got any of that to say anything about it, but... That seems like one of the worst crimes around. Yeah, that's theirs. It is about not quite the worst. It's somewhere around, you know, unless little girls are the worst ones. I think I'm the... I think I don't... Everybody. Uh oh. There's something I don't want to say either.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How's it going to say it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Um, I was reading Ram and the Thicket the other day.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You know, Robinson's book? Frank Robinson's book?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, uh huh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And the picture he paints of the people that lived up around the foothills of Moscow Mountain?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He paints them as being a pretty tough bunch.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He did.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think that was true? They were pretty tough and there were feuds and things like that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Not serious ones. Only one fellow that was bad, really, he is from Tennessee.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: And he wasn't very smart, but he allowed to kill his own. But otherwise... He was the man your father got in the fight with. Like I said, you take... as far as the others was concerned... Well, they were little tough, you know, fist fighters around there. If you know them, if you think they're kind of... They were pretty tough characters that way, you know, if they don't kill a man. Well, then, I think you're darn sure. There was one of them, but his name was...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: But anyway, you know, this guy that cut my dad up...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He had a guy in the Alatam or something, you know, and he was quite an Indian guest. And he sneaked up one day with one of them old big lead guns, you know, black powder. And got up there and this old guy was in the orchard, you know, and was ambushing, you know, for maybe 150 yards or maybe 200, you know. And then the animal gun, you know, you take 40, 65, or 45 or 90, you know. It was big enough, you know, to go miles, but they wouldn't carry much over 50 yards, you know, if you had them sighted in for 50 yards. You'd think, you know, darn thing, shoot, you know, for a quarter of a mile. Anyway, he kept the first shot. He was out in the orchard, this guy, with a kind of a limb right over his head. This old guy, he'd come to see, you know, I guess where he really was mean. He knew his score and he ducked, you know, flattened on the ground. And soon he was cold and everything else that he got out of there and out of sight. He got his own gun, but I guess he didn't get in sight then. That fellow shot two or three times, I think. And finally, that fellow went somewhere, you know, and he got stuck on a school teacher. And I guess he thought he was all right too. He was going to get married and he left the room and of course, if he was right, I heard folks could get that letter, you know, and she'd never get it. So after a while, maybe I told you that before. I don't think so, no. So he went to the school out as far as she was teaching. Dan got a letter from her for a long time and he figured that she'd discarded him, you know. He was part of maybe all of them, I forget this part. I don't know, I don't know, something. But anyway, I got him, he went to school and killed her. As Dan said, he didn't kill that old guy, you know, he probably got a bonus for that. It was pretty off the balance anyway. Had to be, you know, to do a trick like that. The girl she was innocent about, you know, she apparently had thought a lot of it. She never got his letters, you know. You know, there's a lot of stuff like that for somebody who's a source of it or somebody who were doing them a little bit, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would the folks from that sort of the stump ranching Moscow mountain area go into Moscow fairly often?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh yes. Some of them, you know, all would every day. Some of them, you know, go down once a week anyway.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would they get pretty wild in Moscow on like on a Friday or Saturday night?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, very much the wilder ones I guess is right here in Moscow.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh really?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He'd get drunk and he'd go to the hospital and he'd hold his money. He lived out there with John Quist, he finally moved to Palouse. But every time he'd get drunk, you know, and throw him in and buy him ten dollars, he made the breads after a while and he'd pay them Moscow. You know, paid enough fines to pay it. He moved to Palouse and I don't know how he made it over there, whether he kept drinking or not. Maybe he paid Palouse too. Maybe he paid Palouse too. Maybe. Started down here. But, oh there's some, I guess a little tough, I don't know, here in Moscow there's more tough guys right here in Moscow than there was out there though.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: It's pretty darny.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Charlie Ramboll?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Charlie Ramboll. Ramboll. Ramboll, yeah. He was from Missouri. He weighed about 200 pounds. According to his brother, he was just waiting wild cats. He was a big man, always laughing.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: This here brother was telling me about it, you know, out there and it was full of crazy out there, you know. There was a bachelor in there, it was Charlie, his brother. He said that brother was coming to visit him on a Sunday there and was telling him, you know, about, I guess, my old brother there. What he done, you know, and everything you know. That's what he was telling me.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He said the affair just grew up on the back of his neck, his neck, you know. He said his brother just got the biggest kick off, but you hope it doesn't bother him at all. But you know that darn fellow that was crazy, he killed a man in the same asylum. And he'd get in there every once in a while, he'd get in there. But they said that he was the strongest four or five men then. But this old Charlie, he wasn't even that strong. He wasn't a bit scared of him, no. And finally, the same day Charlie was such a scooke of a man, he went over to the potlatch, Logan, or Lumber, Somel, you know. And he lost a leg. And that's the last I ever heard of him. He lost a leg, he got married over there. Had some kids and whatever to come with him. But he was quite a fellow. And then this old Lev Rambo, you know, it was Gander's brother. But that Lev, you know, Yeah, well, he was as honest outside of the gallon as anybody, you know. If you owed your dollar, he paid it. If he told you anything, he kept his word. Old Charlie, though, he was different. He was crooked in every way.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: What kind of things would people do for entertainment when they came in, if they, you know, been working hard like other wood camps, and they've been working hard all week and wanted to come down to have a good time.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind of things would they do?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, they'd go to a dance or something. See, they'd have dances out there. They'd have a dance out there. You know, it was pretty old in there. Somebody would come and fiddle, you know. Yeah, big time. They went to dances out there in the country. In them days, it was an all night affair. They would start, quit at one o 'clock in the night. Went through daylight. Next morning. Oh, it was quite a thing. Of course, there had just been the same as there is now. Because people went to church and didn't go to dances.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Most of the people weren't really mean. Just a few of them thought it was not. You know, of course, that was... Patterson was a little wild. There was a bunch of them. I don't know how many of them. There was one that seemed a little bit... Henry Patterson, that was the oldest of them. And there was John Patterson, he was next to them. And then there was Ernest Patterson, too. And Charlie Patterson. There were about four of them Pattersons. They'd all drink and they'd all fight at the drop of a hat. They'd drop the hat for shows. It was quite a thing, you know. All good colors of them.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: They'd have a fight and get whipped. Just as friendly as they could be. They whipped the other fellas and then tried to run over them. You know, that's one thing that so many people do. If they can whip somebody, they run over them after they've whipped them. You never try to, you know. They would always make a mistake. Like Gully.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Did you hear or seem to think about that fellow by the name of Mike Allen? Whippin' somebody here in Moscow and got a hundred dollar fine?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Uh -uh. I think I know him, but... It's been a long time since I've seen him. He went to Portland last time I seen him. He was... Well, I guess he was mostly Indian, but he was a different kind. You couldn't tell him from the white person, you know.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: His mother... No, I've never seen him. His young mother. You couldn't tell if she was an Indian and you couldn't tell if his father was an Indian. And his sister was an Indian or an Indian, but they overwhelmed him about... ...like a tribe, you know.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Somebody once... somebody told me a couple months back that... ...there used to be whorehouses over on the other side of the tracks.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Whorehouses?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, there was three of them. Not on this side of the track. No, on the other side of the track. Yeah, down... let's see where the Idaho Hotel is. It's been west of there about a couple blocks. There's three whorehouses. One is a string, kind of a circle. Two on one run north and south. And then one on the east side of that. That's 444, that is the name of that one. And this other one is 222 and one is 111.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Huh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were those the addresses or just the names of them?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: There was a number of them.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were they all owned by the same folks?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, each one had different outfits, you know. Uh -huh. See one, Grace Fleming was a landlady of one. I forget what Dylan's name was. There was three landlady's owned by three houses, one in each house. And of course, the other one was a man named John. And that's what the girls are going on telling you now.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were most of the customers single men from back in the hills or from around here?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: By God, I know. The biggest part of their customers was the ones they married, man. Hmm. That's odd. Yeah. Of course, the single ones went to, but... So, you know, they married, man. Quite a few married men went, you know. Of course, there was a kind of funny thing about them days, you know. You know about all my... Maybe they were old ladies that had gotten tired of letting them have as often as they wanted.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He couldn't hear you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I say, oh, maybe I haven't talked to a thing much now. No, it's okay. Really.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where are you going to play them tapes, man?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: We're not going to play them. This is for...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: We're trying to find out information, you know.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, that's all.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You ain't going to play them, do you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, we're not going to put them on the radio or anything. This is just for the information, you know. Oh, yeah. We need to know all this sort of stuff. Well, that's the difference. I could have told you everything. I thought maybe he was going to spread it out. No.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You see that, Captain?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, I don't know where he was, but... I believe it was the last fall, but I'm not sure... ...where that one fella shot himself out there in the woods, Tom Hendricks. I didn't hear about it. He was in that thing about a year ago. And then there was a son of a gun that sent his boy and that other fella to spy on me. Oh! He didn't know that. He shot himself here about a year ago. Well, maybe he felt sorry for doing it. No, but more than that, he... ...he got pulled around. He thought he was a gigolo, I guess. His wife died and he was chasing a wild woman around here. I don't know. I think she killed her husband. She was a little girl. And...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: So then... I think she got in jail. Well, I told him she was coming back to marry him. And he thought what that would be like. You know, it would be like her, you know, shooting her husband. But he left to scare him, you know. So he thought he'd better kill himself, I guess. So he did, and shot himself at a very, very... ...cud me. Well...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were the whorehouses pretty well kept up? Were they pretty clean places and everything?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: They were supposed to be, but... ...you just have to catch a ghost there that it was anywhere.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well... Did the...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: How did the law in the town...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: It was supposed to be then.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, the law? Were they legal then?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: The whorehouses, yeah. They were supposed to be examined every week. Each one of the girls, you know. You just have to catch a ghost there that they'll need. Because there was anywhere you could run loose and wouldn't catch any more ghosts. Right. Because they'd probably pay them off, you know, of course. For examination. But they'd let them go on and... Of course, that made work for the doctors, no, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was this around the prohibition days?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Around that... No, that was before prohibition. I just wide open those days, you see. I tell you how many saloons and things I was in the other times. Well, you see this right there on Third Street is on the other side over there. On the north side of Third Street, there about a second block up from Main, is a fellow named John Olsen had a saloon. And right straight across on Third is a warthorse saloon. They were called Wonder Saloon.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The what?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Wonder Saloon. N -D -E -R. Warthorse. In German. And a little farther up there was a noun. You could say it was sweets. Or Norwegian noun. That was about the same block. So that was Three. Then down on the corner of Third and Main was a saloon. And that was another saloon. A corner saloon. And across there at Moscow Hotel there's a saloon in there. And then north of Third Street there's, I'm sure, one or two saloons up there. And then there's a brewery down there. And then there's a saloon down on Main there below the Moscow Hotel. It wasn't a very big one, it was a small saloon. I don't know, I guess there were about seven or eight saloons all together.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And they all had pretty good business?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, they all had pretty good business. That brewery made the best beer you ever drank.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was it called? What did they call it?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: They didn't have no name.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: For the beer or the brewery?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: No, the brewery didn't.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did the beer have a name?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Nope, not as I ever heard of. It was called Moscow Beer, you know. And there was an old fellow, he was a, you know, man of the era.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was he?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He wasn't a German. He wasn't a Schweitzer.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I believe he was the one who made the beer, boy.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: An Austrian or something?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: It could have been. He was a good honest fellow too.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He was a good what?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I'd say he was a good honest fellow too. Uh huh. You see there was a fellow that lost a leg. Lost a leg. See in them days, money wasn't like this now. He went to him for a donation, you know. He gave $100 for, you know, a beer. You know, because he'd rather pick up the collection, you see. And it began, that later on, when they got probation, you know, he kept trying to run, look like him in a little beer, you know. Turned the best share of the fellow, didn't double cross him and let him catch him. Catch the old boy, you know. Well, he'd give $100 to the cause, you know. Somebody else would take him up. But he didn't give all the money.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You know, can you teach that kind of a deal? Where other stuff would help you turn around?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Because he would have got over $25 for a double crossing. You know, they don't get very much for turning in somebody. I figured, you just get that much and that's it. And there was some pretty wonderful old fellows in Moscow, too, you know. Around, I don't know. Some of the best fellows I ever knew, I think, was... One of the fellows named Bill Harrister, he went from here up to EI. He was kind of fat, you know. And the hardest fellow to laugh, you ever seen, you know. He was a good worker, too. He was a little on the fat. I don't know. He had to love kids, you know, boys. Of course, I suppose he had to. Boys was the main folks, you know. He just loved them now.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, kind of funny. And fish. He was the darndest fisher you ever seen. He wasn't a fly fisher, either. He was just a bait fisher. Yeah, but it really gets... You know, in these streams, them days, you know, there's really some fish in Elk River, you know. Potlatch, you know, it gets... There used to be no trouble at all, I guess, in Elk River to catch trout up to 18 inches. And well, in Potlatch, too, in Potlatch River, you know, and forts and things. I've done a lot of fishing back in Elk River. And the first time I ever went to Elk River, my dad's team took us...

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Maybe I ain't sure just how long it took us. I only took a couple of days to get there, you know, with the bacon. And we got over there and we didn't have no bait and we didn't know nothing about fishing in the first place. Just had some plain hooks and some line. Couldn't find any bait. It was dry in the summertime. So, caught one and found a fly hook. Still, I didn't know anything about fly fishing or anything. I didn't know anything about this hook. I just found it on the bank, personally. I had a change in hooks or something. I left it and I caught one in case of that. We got up to the top of the hill there. There was an old barn or something. I had a couple of young boys come in there the night before, you know. They had 20 gallons worth of gags in there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Worth 20 gallons worth of what?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Gags, you know. A whole 20 gallon of fish. They said the poor night did have them all full. And the inside was clean of them. There was a patient that was selling them. Of course, then there wasn't anything to prohibit them from selling them. There was so many patients.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: I know there was one other fellow.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you ever do any fly fishing?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: A little bit. He had 20 hooks on one outfit. But I never seen him fishing. His kid was a showrunner to us. He was out in the creek, but there was no big chance. The kids had to all go out. He was a showrunner to us. I just wondered what he was doing. He had that in the ochre at that time. About the same time we went there and caught one fish out of the ochre. You need a little bait to catch them, usually. On this side of the ochre, there was what they called Bull Run. They had a lot of fish in the creek there. The fish in there were in the same town where somebody caught some fish. Cleaning their eyes was in the hedge. We used the eyes and we caught some fish there, but not too many. Probably 15 or 20. Around the center of the brush, most of the time it was going in. We'd have had them eyes over there at the ochre. Probably caught a fish big enough to pull us in.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: When we'd done something there, we'd have any brains. We'd never done it. There was a pole ridge. There was some of them right about. We drove a team across there. The water was deep in, too. We got tired of it. Drove a team across there and hooked them over there. We stayed all night over there on the outside. The next morning, we went back. Obviously, it was going about four days, I guess.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: We had some of them eyes for bait. We were there. It made a difference in catching fish. Another thing we didn't know was where to fish. Instead of fishing the ripples, we was trying to fish the hole in the where is clear and big and deep. That's the right way to fish.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were you young then?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, yes. I don't know. That's probably 22 or 23.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where did people do their gambling around here mostly? The gambling?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, there was an outfit there. That one, the saloon there, they had a place back there. A separate room. They'd play in there. Of course, you see, what they got out of it, they paid the saloon keeper. For instance, them guys were professional gamblers. Marked cards. Of course, they had them marked in the saloon deck. New deck of cards. Call for new deck of cards. Somebody would get them.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: It's the same old thing that would be marked.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Wouldn't it be kind of hard for professional gamblers around here? Wouldn't people catch on pretty soon that they were being cheated?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Heck no.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Some of them weren't smart enough to catch on and they didn't gamble. But the others, you know, the farmers, you know, there's several farmers lost, you know. Piece by piece, you know. Several cops and then they'd get meat and mortgages on their face. A lot of red houses built from gamblers, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were there many professional gamblers here?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, there was. Professional, you know, they had to mark cards and everything, you know. Knowing how to play them was one thing about it too, you know. Of course, I'd seen a lot of gamblers, card, but this one Lev. Him and I was up there in the...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The one who?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Lev Rambo. You see the brothers of that husky fellow that I was telling you about, that Charlie. He was up there in the camp, the logging camp. He'd mark the cards with his knife, you know, just a little peek. And then, boy, he'd take them every night.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Lev did that?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. But if he owed you a dollar, he'd pay it. Ten dollars he'd pay it or more. He was honest except he would cease in the car.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you used to play with Lev?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah. I didn't lose anything.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were you sort of partners with him? You went to the logging camp?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, I'd start the game and he'd finish it. Oh.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: You know, like I had two and a half I wanted to play in. I'd tell them, well, you put... Some of us get this, I'd say, you're done for the night. Of course, I'd quit, you see. Because that's all, you know. You'd see them doggone little specks, you know, flying in the air. Uh -huh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, you'd go into the bunkhouse or something and get a game started?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, right there in the main bunkhouse.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where would Lev be then?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: He'd be right there in the bunkhouse too. Oh. See, I was the one, you know, backpacking on the play cards. Uh -huh. He was a lapper, quite a lapper himself like the other two. There was one guy, I don't know, I won't say what his nationality was, but he thought he was pretty smart, you know. Uh -huh. And he lost all of his money, some of it, before we got there. Uh -huh. And he had it coming, you see, because it wasn't making only about $18 a month, clear above the board. Uh -huh. But he got in there, you know, several different times, you know, and playing in solitary, you know, with that deck of cards. Of course, you take a new deck in there, and they wouldn't play for that at all, you know, on that old leathery deck, you know. Uh -huh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did they want that old leathery deck?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Well, they figured that, you know, it wasn't marked. Oh. They didn't know anything about it, it wasn't marked anyway. Uh -huh. But they figured, you know, by being played so long, they couldn't see nothing. I seen this one fella, he, you know, was playing solitary, you know, and he'd made this ramble one every night, you know. Uh -huh. So he told some of the fellas, you know, that's why he didn't have his name, and said, maybe you can play on paper. That's what they called the marked cards, you know, on paper. And he ripped them out of the way that night. He had his month's cash, $18, and he spent the whole darn thing in about two hours. He was so smart, you know, he thought he was.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Uh -huh. New deck, they wouldn't have been with us at all. Uh -huh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, after you'd start the game, then you'd quit, would you lose money?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Would you go ahead and... Oh, yeah, I'd usually lose. That's the money that I took out.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: To start the game with. To start the game with, yeah. I'd usually lose on purpose, you know. Right. Push the ramble.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Then Lev would split with you or something?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Oh, heck, he'd never done. Uh -huh. He was honest, the fella, in that line that I'd ever seen, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I was telling you, you know, you know, you know something about that double crossbow, didn't you?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: A little bit. It kind of confused me, though. You told to me last time, I got kind of confused about how it worked.

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, I only seen cold decking.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Cold decking?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: Yeah, and signed me. Well, I was going to win, really. I signed his hand, and they thought it was a cold deck, you see.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, what do you mean by cold decking?

speaker: Ulysses Showalter
words: That's running decking on them, you see. Have him, well, say like here, you know, maybe in a hotel room, you know. That's where we'd all take them. And have a bed here, you know, and have just two chairs. One like this, one like that, a straight back chair over there. That would be the one that said the man's going to win, you know, really.
